article_title: Stanford Engineers Crack Single-Molecule Protein Sequencing by Reverse-Translating Peptides into DNA

content_preview.title: Stanford Engineers Crack Single-Molecule Protein Sequencing by Reverse-Translating Peptides into DNA
content_preview.summary: Stanford bioengineers publish a reverse-translation chemistry in Nature Biotechnology that converts peptides into DNA barcodes, achieving single-molecule resolution up to 1,000 times more sensitive than mass spectrometry.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
A team at Stanford University has developed a method that converts protein sequences into DNA, allowing standard high-throughput DNA sequencers to read peptides at single-molecule resolution. The technique, published March 18 in [Nature Biotechnology](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03061-z), overcomes longstanding sensitivity limits of mass spectrometry and opens a path toward routine, large-scale protein analysis from minimal biological samples.

recommendations: 2 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Thorough technical reporting on a significant biotech breakthrough. Clear explanation of the reverse-translation chemistry, practical sensitivity advantages, and honest assessment of remaining challenges.
editor_notes.source_verification: 2 of 3 sources manually verified. Phys.org returned 403 but claims were confirmed via Stanford Report metadata. Nature Biotechnology article confirmed via DOI (s41587-026-03061-z). Stanford News confirmed Liwei Zheng quote about mass spectrometry sensitivity (1000x improvement), H. Tom Soh push-button instrument vision. Patent and competing interests disclosures match article.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Key technical claims verified: reverse translation of peptides into DNA, Edman degradation basis, proximity extension assays, 1000x sensitivity vs mass spectrometry, Nature Biotechnology March 18 publication, Stanford authorship. The 40% TPV efficiency claim matches MIT News reporting for the Fourth Power article (different context). Author names and affiliations confirmed.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Balanced scientific reporting. Appropriately notes limitations in the final section (controlled conditions, antibody panel scaling needed).
editor_notes.originality: Clear, accessible explanation of complex biochemistry without dumbing down the science.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality science news reporting with verified claims and appropriate caveats. Approved.
